rulings.Case Title: MATS NAHLINDER VS. MARKMONITOR, INC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Markmonitor, Inc.'S Motion For Protective Order Limiting Scope Of Plaintiff'S Noticed Depositions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, January 4, 2013, at 9:00 am, line 5, Defendant Markmonitor, Inc.'S Motion For Protective Order Limiting Scope Of Plaintiff'S Noticed Depositions. Pro Tem Judge Ernst Halperin, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: HEARING REQUIRED. It would be helpful to the court if the parties were prepared to address their respective positions on whether the comparator information sought that consists of other employees' contracts and commission payments meet the threshold standard of discoverability under the Code of Civil Procedure Section 2017.010. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GIS PLANNING INC. et al VS. MARCOS MONZON
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Otion For Order Rendering Judgment By Default
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, January 4, 2013, at 9:00 am, line 3, PLAINTIFFs GIS PLANNING INC. and PABLO MONZON's MOtion For Order Rendering Judgment By Default. Pro Tem Judge Ernst Halperin, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Denied without prejudice. The Court sets a hearing for January 25, 2012 at 9:00 a.m. in Department 302 for an order to show cause why terminating sanctions should not be granted in the event defendant has not fully complied with the court's order entered October 1, 2012 before the hearing date. Any brief or response is limited to 5 pages, plus documentary evidence, and shall be filed and served in accordance with CCP § 1005(b). Courtesy copies are to be provided to Department 302 at the time of filing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to ernst@ernsthalperin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: QING LIU MO VS. LILLIAN MOCK et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Moiton To Quash Subpoenas And Request For Protective Order;
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, January 4, 2013, at 9:00 am, line 4, DEFENDANTs LILLIAN MOCK & LU MOCK's Motion To Quash Subpoenas And Request For Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge Ernst Halperin, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: GRANTED IN PART. However, a hearing is required. Defendants have not shown that emails defendants sent or received through their workplace email accounts constitute confidential personnel files within a zone of privacy recognized in Board of Trustees v Superior Court (1981) 119 Cal. Ap. 3d 516. However, the subpoenas, as written, are grossly overbroad and would capture emails that constitute business records of the employers that have nothing to do with this litigation. The parties are to present proposed search terms at the hearing that would not capture business records of the employers. =(302/JPT)
